main_summary.summary: William Lamar Strickland, interviewed by Joe Mosnier at New Africa House, University of Massachusetts Amherst on September 23, 2011, traces a movement biography that begins in Boston's Roxbury and extends through national Black radical politics. Born January 4, 1937, Strickland attended Boston Latin School and Harvard, served in the Marine Corps from 1956 to 1959 (including a posting in Albany, Georgia), and returned to Harvard for graduate work with Erik H. Erikson. He recounts a childhood acquaintance with Malcolm Little through his cousin Leslie, who shared time with Malcolm at Charlestown State Prison, and a later reconnection with Malcolm X at Harvard and in Harlem. Strickland joined the Northern Student Movement under founder Peter Countryman and succeeded him as executive director, expanding city projects in Boston, Hartford, New York, Detroit, and Chicago. He worked with William Kunstler and Arthur Kinoy organizing congressional support for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party's 1965 challenge brought by Fannie Lou Hamer, Victoria Gray Adams, and Annie Devine, with Representative William Fitts Ryan introducing the floor resolution. Strickland joined Vincent Harding at the Institute of the Black World in Atlanta, taught at UMass Amherst, served as chief consultant on the PBS Malcolm X documentary Make It Plain, and discusses Frantz Fanon, Tanzania, Walter Rodney, and the Black Power conferences.
key_themes: Malcolm X's critique of American racism
Northern Student Movement and community organizing
Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party congressional challenge
Institute of the Black World and Black Studies
Vincent Harding and the convergence of Martin and Malcolm
main_summary.historical_significance:
Strickland's testimony documents the institutional architecture of Northern Black movement work that is often subordinated in standard SNCC- and SCLC-centered narratives: the Northern Student Movement's tenant organizing with Jesse Gray in Harlem, the legal-congressional substrate of the MFDP challenge organized with William Kunstler and Arthur Kinoy, and the founding of the Institute of the Black World in Atlanta as the first independent Black think tank in U.S. history. His firsthand recollections of Malcolm X in Roxbury, Harlem, and at Harvard, and his account of the Charlestown State Prison overlap between his cousin Leslie and Malcolm Little, provide primary-source corroboration of personal-history details that recent biographies have contested.

The interview also preserves Strickland's documentary record as chief consultant on the PBS Make It Plain (1994) and as a writer who responded publicly to Manning Marable's 2011 Malcolm X biography, situating him as a custodian of Malcolm X interpretive scholarship across four decades. His reflections on Vincent Harding's evolving role at the King Center, the development of Black Studies curricula, and his Marine Corps formation as a Black serviceman in the late 1950s Jim Crow South add structural detail to multiple parallel threads in the Civil Rights History Project corpus.

chapters.title: False Face to America, We Have Found You Out
chapters.summary: William Lamar Strickland is introduced by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of Massachusetts in Amherst on September 23, 2011, for the Civil Rights History Project, and opens by crediting Worth Long, the poet of SNCC, with the line falsely attributed to Stokely Carmichael, false face to America, we have found you out.

chapters.title: Six of Us Were Black
chapters.summary: Strickland recounts a teacher steering him to apply to Boston Latin School, where of nearly 700 boys only about 30 graduated and six were Black, and traces his mother's family to land near Macon, Georgia, in a town called Haddock, inherited from a Confederate-era plantation owner, Madison Gray, who left part of his land to the family.

chapters.title: The Man Too Mean to Die
chapters.topic: Family and How He Met Malcolm
chapters.summary: Strickland says his father was killed in the Italian campaign in World War II and his mother worked for Raytheon, recalls a West Indian uncle, Jake Edman, a numbers banker he called the man too mean to die, and tells how his cousin Leslie landed in Charlestown State Prison at the same time as Malcolm X, so that when Malcolm later spoke at Harvard, Strickland mentioned Roxbury and Leslie and the two stayed close until Malcolm died.

chapters.title: Eleven of Us at Harvard
chapters.topic: Harvard and the Secret of Writing
chapters.summary: Strickland describes being one of only eleven Black students at Harvard, a tight nurturing community told to prove they were geniuses and not merely somebody's son, invokes Du Bois failing freshman English there, and recalls his teacher Mr. Russo telling him the secret of good writing is rewriting.

chapters.title: Free by '63
chapters.topic: NAACP Youth Council and Race Awareness
chapters.summary: Graduating Boston Latin in 1954 around the time of Brown, Strickland recalls his NAACP Youth Council involvement and its Free by '63 slogan, his uncle Gray who fled Georgia after beating a white man, and being admitted at Harvard into a graduate seminar with C. Eric Lincoln while attending Temple No. 11 to hear Louis X, drawn to Malcolm X's worldview.

chapters.title: Take Your Behind to Cambridge
chapters.summary: Strickland recounts nearly accepting a scholarship to Howard after seeing a campus full of beautiful women, his friend Carl McCall who went to Dartmouth, and his mother and community urging him toward Harvard, in a Roxbury where the whole community knew your accomplishments and held you responsible to the race, told to be twice as good as white competitors.

chapters.title: Albany, Georgia, Not Albany, New York
chapters.summary: Strickland tells how he assumed Marine orders to Albany meant New York but found they meant Albany, Georgia, calls the Corps a formative encounter with white America, a fundamentally Southern institution of officers and enlisted men many sent by a judge choosing jail or the Marines, and recalls catching himself humming the Everly Brothers at Parris Island and Camp Lejeune, a lesson in how an environment seeps into your consciousness.

chapters.title: The Picture in Jet
chapters.topic: Emmett Till and Northern Student Movement
chapters.summary: Back at Harvard in the late 1950s, Strickland names the murder of Emmett Till, its picture displayed in Jet, as what struck his generation, recalls Howard Mumford Jones assigning Richard Wright's Uncle Tom's Children and Baldwin's Notes of a Native Son, and his joining the Northern Student Movement, founded by Yale undergraduate Peter Countryman with city projects across the country.

chapters.title: You Belonged to Everybody
chapters.topic: His Mother and a Lost Community
chapters.summary: Asked to say more about his mother, Strickland calls her influence incalculable and says she devoted her life to him, then mourns the loss of the dense Walnut Avenue community of Jewish, Irish, and Armenian neighbors and shared churches, contrasted with Grace Boggs telling him organizers can no longer safely walk Detroit's streets.

chapters.title: You Didn't Have to Teach People to Be Black
chapters.summary: Strickland argues the younger generation is ahistorical through no fault of its own, lacking the Black newspapers and radio stations that gave continuity, with five corporations controlling the media, and describes new confusion over gender, sexual orientation, and identity among Black people from Africa and the West Indies who lack America's history.

chapters.title: Religious When You're Being Shot At
chapters.summary: Strickland names his Boston church as Charles Street AME and says he is not particularly spiritual, joking that you become religious only when being shot at, but that he enjoys the church experience and will respond to a good preacher independent of whether he believes the theology.

chapters.title: The Secret of Organizing
chapters.topic: Jesse Gray and the Rent Strikes
chapters.summary: Returning to Northern Student Movement work in an office near Harlem, Strickland honors Jesse Gray, the Harlem rent-strike leader and former merchant seaman who introduced him to the history of Black communists and taught that the secret of organizing is convincing people to organize themselves, collecting and banking rent money during strikes against landlords.

chapters.title: A Bad Motorcycle
chapters.summary: Strickland says SNCC was a major influence on Northern Student Movement's turn toward community organizing, and tells of Detroit's Kenny Cockrel, an organizer with General Baker who became a lawyer able to rap almost like Malcolm X, won acquittals for a Black policewoman and a Black autoworker, then resigned from the Detroit City Council as duplicitous before dying of a heart attack in his early fifties.

chapters.title: He Never Tried to Convert Me
chapters.summary: Relocating to New York in 1963, Strickland describes Malcolm X being everywhere, holding rallies on 125th Street near the Hotel Theresa alongside figures like Michaux, and recalls that although Malcolm visited his Boston home and they reconnected at Harvard, he never tried to convert Strickland, who would not give up spare ribs and chasing girls to join the Nation.

chapters.title: The Fundamental Critic of American Racism
chapters.summary: Strickland frames Malcolm X as the fundamental critic of American racism the way Marx critiqued capitalism, as hard on Black people as on white, exposing how they had been brainwashed and reframing the sit-ins, and a prodigious reader of several papers and magazines a day whom he once caught reading William F. Buckley's National Review because you never can tell where you come across a good idea.

chapters.title: And There Was No New Law
chapters.summary: Strickland recounts Malcolm X's point that when Bull Connor used fire hoses Kennedy claimed he could not intervene, but nationalized the Guard once Birmingham's Black masses erupted after the bombing of the A.G. Gaston Motel and A.D. King's house in May 1963, and that the FBI merely took notes while SNCC workers were arrested and after Schwerner, Chaney, and Goodman J. Edgar Hoover said civil rights workers could not be protected.

chapters.title: E.T. Comes Down From Space
chapters.summary: Strickland relays Malcolm X teaching from Dwight Lowell Dumond's Antislavery and using the slave power and broken Indian treaties to make people rethink what they were taught, posing the thought experiment of an extraterrestrial learning that fifteen of the sixteen presidents before Lincoln were slave owners and that all four hundred treaties with the Indians were broken.

chapters.title: A Media Cocoon
chapters.summary: Strickland describes pressing his students on how the media brainwashes them, contrasting on-site Arab Spring coverage from Tunisia and Egypt with the absence of coverage from Iraq, Afghanistan, or Gaza, and recounting the protests against George W. Bush abroad, including a London visit so unwelcome the Lord Mayor compared him to William of Orange, none of it shared with the American people.

chapters.title: Congress Makes Its Own Laws
chapters.topic: The Freedom Vote and the Challenge
chapters.summary: Strickland explains the move from sit-ins to the vote as Robert Parris Moses went to Mississippi, the 1963 Freedom Vote, and the MFDP challenging three white congressmen by nominating Fannie Lou Hamer, Victoria Gray Adams, and Annie Devine, and credits William Fitts Ryan, who put forward the resolution forcing the white congressmen to step aside, drawing fifty or sixty members to rise and a vote of 149, short of a majority but enough to show how crooked the system was.

chapters.title: Washing the Blood From His Shirt
chapters.topic: The Humbling of Fannie Lou Hamer
chapters.summary: Strickland says the deepest impact of the MFDP was not political but sitting with Fannie Lou Hamer and grasping that her clarity about the world was infinitely superior to all his book learning, and recounts Bob Moses, beaten while taking people to register near Amzie Moore, washing the blood from his shirt so it would not scare the people.

chapters.title: In Case I Got Killed
chapters.summary: Strickland explains he married a woman he met in New York partly to partake of life in case he was killed in Mississippi, and recalls being at a Northern Student Movement meeting upstate on February 21, 1965, when word came that Malcolm X had been killed, an irreplaceable loss of someone who embodied the urban Northern Black person as Martin embodied the South.

chapters.part: Black Studies and the Life of the Mind
chapters.title: Black Heritage on CBS
chapters.topic: The First Black Studies on Television
chapters.summary: Strickland describes putting together the television program Black Heritage: A History of Afro-Americans on CBS with Vincent Harding and John Henrik Clarke, the first representation of black studies on television, aired by CBS at five or six in the morning, later linked to figures Harding gathered when Coretta Scott King asked him to direct the King Center.

chapters.part: Black Studies and the Life of the Mind
chapters.title: From Freud to Fanon
chapters.summary: Strickland tells how he found a new hero in Frantz Fanon, plunging into the Algerian war and the broader history of colonialism, including French African troops barred from marching in the liberation of Paris, and teaches his students that history is not abstract dates but the flow of how things are connected from A to B to C to D.

chapters.part: Black Studies and the Life of the Mind
chapters.title: Are You One, Two?
chapters.summary: Strickland recounts studying with Erik Erikson and Edwin Boring at Harvard intending to become a psychoanalyst out of love for Freud, a non-directive-therapy session at a Waltham mental hospital with a Black World War I veteran driven mad by racism, and his switch from Freud to Fanon and liberation as the field moved from psychoanalysis to drug therapy.

chapters.title: He Turned Us Around
chapters.topic: Selma and a Skeptical View of King
chapters.summary: Strickland recalls meeting Martin Luther King a few times without being impressed, being on the third Selma march, captured on the Northern Student Movement bus in the Eyes on the Prize episode Bridge to Freedom, and how learning a deal had been made when King turned the marchers around did not enhance his view, which only later shifted through researching with a colleague who had been in Albany with King.

chapters.title: Martin Was Converging Toward Malcolm
chapters.summary: Through teaching on Martin and Malcolm X and reading Garrow's Bearing the Cross, Strickland gained admiration for King, citing King's statement six months before his assassination that the whole thing must be done away with, the same disillusionment Black Power and most movement people went through, and asking why so many were sympathetic to Elijah Muhammad's description of the white man as the devil.

chapters.part: The Institute of the Black World
chapters.title: Go Organize Your Mama
chapters.summary: Strickland tells how a colleague brought him to the Institute of the Black World in Atlanta after the formal movement died, amid Pan-Africanism and the name-changing he found ridiculous, recalls debating Stokely Carmichael's All-African People's Revolutionary Party by insisting the struggle was here, and a Howard concert bringing Walter Rodney and Garvey scholar Robert Hill.

chapters.part: The Institute of the Black World
chapters.title: How Are the Green Bay Packers Doing
chapters.summary: Strickland recounts Walter Rodney barred by Jamaica and Guyana and teaching at the University of Dar es Salaam, his own 1971 or 1972 visit to a Tanzania idolizing Nyerere, and Black American expatriates there who never learned the language and asked how the Green Bay Packers were doing, a lesson reinforced in Cuba about how deeply your own society has socialized you.

chapters.part: The Institute of the Black World
chapters.title: We Ain't Doing That
chapters.topic: IBW Splits From the King Center
chapters.summary: Strickland explains that the Institute of the Black World refused the King Center advisors' wish to glorify Martin and take an oath of nonviolence, becoming independent with newsletters and students kicked out of Morehouse, and names the faculty drawn to UMass, Ekwueme Michael Thelwell, Chester Davis, and others he knew from SNCC, who invited him to take their places.

chapters.part: The Institute of the Black World
chapters.title: Ninety Bucks to Atlanta
chapters.summary: Strickland describes commuting between New York and Atlanta for ninety dollars to teach, naming George Napper who became Atlanta police commissioner, Howard Dodson who became IBW director, and Jan Douglass and Pat Daly who ran it until the Institute of the Black World ended in 1983.

chapters.title: A Left Nationalist by Way of Malcolm
chapters.summary: Responding to Derrick White's history of IBW describing him as a student of Malcolm X and a political nationalist, Strickland says if he is anything he is a Malcolm X man, recounts Manning Marable calling him and James Turner left nationalists, and frames black studies through an African fable about who runs the school and Baldwin and Malcolm X as the two sharpest critics of American contradictions.

chapters.title: There Is a River
chapters.summary: Strickland names Vincent Harding a tremendous influence, citing his history There Is a River, a planned WETA series preempted when Roots aired and one Black show was deemed enough so Harding published the work as The Other American Revolution, and Harding's book on Martin Luther King as the inconvenient hero whose last five years were sent down the memory hole.

chapters.title: Every Rebellion Was a Response
chapters.summary: Strickland says the surveillance of Malcolm X reached MI5 and Mossad and that the police are the military arm of the racist state, argues nearly every urban rebellion answered an act of police racism, and describes the purposeful targeting of exploitative merchants in Detroit in 1967 and after Newark, with the National Guard and media smashing everything to portray the revolts as mindless.

chapters.title: Why Malcolm X Was a Great Man
chapters.summary: Strickland describes how Make It Plain grew from the Eyes on the Prize episode The Time Has Come, the most-responded-to episode, leading Henry Hampton's Blackside to partner with PBS, his role as chief consultant, and his being asked to write the companion book's introduction to describe as objectively as he could why Malcolm X was a great man.

chapters.title: Re-Invention as Method
chapters.summary: Strickland summarizes his published critique of Manning Marable's Malcolm X biography as full of historical omissions, gaps, and unverified speculation, argues the re-invention trope places agency in the interpreter and could be applied nefariously to anyone, and notes the assassins were already named by Talmadge Hayer and in Karl Evanzz's The Judas Factor, from whom Marable borrowed heavily without attribution.

chapters.part: No More White Girls in the Ghetto
chapters.title: The First Racial Division of Labor
chapters.summary: Strickland recounts good early relations with SDS, their ERAP turn to community organizing in Newark with Junius Williams and Stanley Aronowitz, and the parting of ways when he removed white women from Northern Student Movement's Harlem organizing after finding them in basements drinking wine and singing Wobbly songs, exposing the people to a life they could never have.

chapters.part: No More White Girls in the Ghetto
chapters.title: Are You Crazy?
chapters.topic: White Allies and the Limits of Love
chapters.summary: Strickland tells of a white woman in Mississippi wanting to hold his hand at a voter registration as a symbol of togetherness, which he saw as a way to get them both killed because white people do not understand what other white people are capable of, and argues you need not love those marching beside you against the bomb, separating the wheat from the chaff among well-meaning white supporters.

chapters.title: The Quintessential Anti-Bush
chapters.summary: Asked about Barack Obama's election, Strickland calls it a potential turning point he did not get ecstatic about, noting the same share of white people who backed McCain had backed Bush in 2004 and that Obama won only because Black voters turned out heavily in Virginia, Florida, and North Carolina, while the world saw him as the quintessential anti-Bush.

chapters.title: Necessary but No Longer Sufficient
chapters.topic: Black Studies and a Corrupt System
chapters.summary: Strickland paraphrases Stokely that black studies is necessary but no longer sufficient because all are on a ship called America heading to hell, citing corporations paying a small share of the federal budget while the masses pay most through income and payroll taxes, the fifteen trillion dollar debt, exported jobs, and laws written by lobbying interests rather than the public.

chapters.title: The Foxes Are in the Hen House
chapters.summary: Strickland argues the regulators are captured, the foxes in the SEC, NLRB, and FAA, points to a graduate student earning twenty-two thousand dollars audited while the wealthy escape and the SEC destroyed investigation documents, and asks why there is no revolution and no opposition to explain the problems, naming a climate-denying Rick Perry while much of Texas burns.

chapters.part: What a Real Confrontation Would Take
chapters.title: You Don't Drink Wine With the People You Organize
chapters.topic: Discipline, the Panthers, and the Police
chapters.summary: Returning to why he moved white women out of urban organizing, Strickland says you do not drink wine or sing Wobbly songs with the people you organize, criticizes the radical-chic lionizing of the Black Panthers by the white and European left, and warns that confronting the police, as an ex-Marine, means understanding what you are doing rather than announcing it, as friends killed like Fred Hampton and Mark Clark show.

chapters.part: What a Real Confrontation Would Take
chapters.title: The Ballot, the Bullet, and Algeria
chapters.summary: Strickland says America admits only little blips and never that the whole thing must be done away with, as Martin came to say, and that Malcolm X was working out how to really change the country through a national strategy of the ballot or the bullet joined to international struggle, invited with Che to a conference in Algiers and conveniently killed two and a half weeks before leaving for Algeria.

chapters.part: What a Real Confrontation Would Take
chapters.title: Once Upon a Time
chapters.summary: Thanked for the long interview, Strickland offers final thanks, regrets the many people he should have mentioned, and says being lucky enough to meet great people and explain their significance, as he tried to do with Malcolm X, is what moved him to write a memoir called Once Upon a Time, closing the Library of Congress and Smithsonian presentation.

loc_video.caption: William Lamar Strickland oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Amherst, Massachusetts, 2011 September 23
